summary: Joel introduces his article on software scheduling by observing that most programmers avoid creating schedules entirely. Those who do create them often do so halfheartedly under management pressure, resulting in schedules nobody believes in. He draws a humorous parallel between management's simultaneous belief in unreliable schedules and UFOs. The post serves primarily as a teaser for his longer article 'Painless Software Schedules.'
key_points: The vast majority of programmers try to avoid making schedules altogether
When schedules are created under duress, nobody actually believes them
There's a contradiction in management simultaneously relying on schedules while accepting that software projects are never on time
money_quotes: This is something almost no programmer wants to do.
Nobody actually believes the schedule except for upper management, which simultaneously believes that "no software project is ever on time" and in the existence of UFOs.
key_insight: Software scheduling fails because programmers resist doing it and management doesn't truly believe in the results, creating a cycle of dysfunction that can be broken with better practices.
